content.full_text:
McDONOUGH, HOLLAND & ALLEN
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
ATTORNEYS

555 CAPITOL MALL, SUITE 950
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 444-3900

IRIS P YANG

TELECOPIER (916) 444-8334

YUBA CITY OFFICE
(585 BUTTE HOUSE ROAD
YUBA CITY, CALIFORNIA 95991
(916) 674-9796
TELECOPIER (916) 671-0990

BAY AREA OFFICE
1999 HARRISON STREET, SUITE 1300
OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612
(510) 444-7500
TELECOPIER (415) 839-9104

January 23, 1990

Kathryn Donovan
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Conflict of Interest

Dear Ms. Donovan:

I am writing to you on behalf of the City of Half Moon Bay and one of its city councilmembers regarding certain decisions to be made in the coming months on a proposed redevelopment plan for the city. I am requesting formal written advice pursuant to Section 83114(b) of the Government Code.

This letter supplements a previous letter sent to you by Councilmember Iverson on January 19, 1990, and the facts stated herein should be considered in conjunction with the information provided in Councilmember Iverson's letter.

The city has initiated proceedings to adopt a redevelopment plan for the North Wavecrest Redevelopment Project. The project area for the proposed Redevelopment Plan includes, in part, the entire downtown commercial area and two small residential neighborhoods of the city. It is anticipated that the plan may include public infrastructure improvements for the residential areas in the Project Area. In addition, the plan may provide for loans or other types of assistance to residential property owners and tenants in these areas for rehabilitation or other types of private improvements.

The city has a total population of approximately 9,000 persons and is less than 10 square miles in size. The proposed project area represents about 10 or 15 percent of the existing residential area of the city.

Kathryn Donovan
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
January 23, 1990
Page 2


Councilmember David Iverson owns a home within one of the
residential areas included in the proposed redevelopment project area. As
the owner of an interest in real property within the project area, may
Councilman Iverson participate in decisions on the adoption of the proposed
redevelopment plan?

We would appreciate your response as quickly as possible. If you have
any questions, please feel free to call me.

Very truly yours,

Iris P. Yang
Iris P. Yang

IPY:pjp

cc:   David Iverson
Mark Weiss
Robert Lanzone


McDONOUGH HOLLAND & ALLEN
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

460 Poplar Street
Half Moon Bay, California 94019
April 10, 1990

California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE: YOUR FILE NUMBER A-90-067

Dear Members Of The Commission:

I understand that in correspondence dated February 26, 1990, Mr. Jonathan S. Rothman of your Legal Division provided written guidance to a Sacramento law firm regarding my eligibility to vote on redevelopment matters in Half Moon Bay -- guidance which was based upon such facts as had been presented to him.

Unfortunately, not all facts were presented to Mr. Rothman.

This letter places many omitted facts before you, and seeks your help, in the form of a Commission opinion, in protecting my electoral constituency from a virulent strain of political disenfranchisement that was never intended nor envisioned by the electorate of California when it enacted, by initiative, the Political Reform Act of 1974.

FACTS

There is always the danger, for development interests, that pointed questions put to those interests by an elected official -- if that official is allowed to speak -- will result in important decisions being made in that greatest of all political disinfectants: the sunshine.

The recent result of Half Moon Bay's dominance by development interests has been (1) unsightly high-density development -- as any visual inspection of Half Moon Bay would readily confirm, (2) proposed high-density development of Half Moon Bay's coastal bluffs -- development on such a scale as would inexorably overwhelm the city's existing infrastructure, notwithstanding expensive "see nothing, hear nothing, speak nothing" studies and "reviews" that are about to be undertaken by hired "experts", and (3) brazen political strategies in furtherance of high-density development.

Aggressive development presents extremely serious issues for the voters of any town. All too often, developers, their advisers, and cooperative city officials are nowhere to be found when it finally becomes apparent that development has necessitated further additions to schools, highways, etc..

Travel into Half Moon Bay on existing roads is already severely restricted by existing traffic loads, with substantial highway relief nowhere in sight. Other future infrastructure concerns abound, as vested interests proceed to push quick development schemes.

At this very time when informed city decisions require disinterested advice without even the appearance of bias, at least some of Half Moon Bay's key redevelopment advisers are ultimately being funded by certain development interests, under a reimbursement arrangement with the city.

At this very time when informed city decisions require disinterested and pointed questioning on the part of elected city officials, my home was rather interestingly included in a proposed "redevelopment" study area", and the FPPC was contacted, resulting in preliminary guidance that I was ineligible to participate in redevelopment and related growth issues. Interestingly, this removed from those growth deliberations the only Half Moon Bay city council member who was beginning to ask pointed questions.

Half Moon Bay citizens have not been receiving the forthright answers they deserve regarding the impact of the

FPPC

- 2 -

April 10, 1990

proposed high-density development on their taxes, traffic, schools, life styles, property values, etc..

For example, some city advisers early on suggested that the use of a redevelopment agency in Half Moon Bay
would have no adverse tax impact on citizens living outside of such redevelopment area -- which turned out to
be "not entirely true", when the town citizenry began to investigate this subject.

Against a backdrop of pressures for quick development, I ran for office and was elected on November 7, 1989
on a platform which was unique among the other city council candidates, to put it mildly. My platform
emphasized balanced, sober, and sane growth, emphasizing the preservation of our region's natural beauties,
within an overall context of sound development.

Indeed, I am not against growth per se. What I am against is the further destruction of those qualities which
made our coastal area what it once was. What I am in favor of is reasonable concessions for our town's citizens
from any would-be developer.

In short, my candidacy addressed some very disturbing problems, given recent happenings on your and my
California coast.

As you may know, California's increasingly controversial Community Redevelopment Law enables a city to form
a redevelopment agency, condemn all property within a subsequently determined redevelopment area, and then
sell such condemned property to favored development interests in a below-market, non-bid, sweetheart deal, with
local taxpayers outside of the redevelopment area and competing cities being the losers.

The potential, in this process, for destruction of the integrity, or appearance of integrity, of our local governments
can be seen from the magnitude of the subsidies that are possible under existing law. In the case of one very
small subsidy, the City of Pacific's redevelopment agency recently sold to a developer for $60,000 a property
it had acquired for $176,000. In the case of much larger deals, the potential for abuse rises exponentially.

Developers often urge this "re"development agency technique upon a city as a low-cost -- to them -- means of
eliminating small landowners in the areas they want to develop (-- not redevelop). In these situations, the about
to be subsidized developers usually protest that their profit margins will be too low in the absence of subsidies.

Given that development project sizes in California are often well into the tens and even hundreds of millions of
dollars, the potential for money-fed destruction of our political processes should be incredibly apparent to all
except the most naive -- a situation which the California Legislature should immediately put on its remedial
agenda.

Under the Community Redevelopment Law, (1) redevelopment cannot proceed in the absence of a legally
sustainable finding of "blight", and (2) areas of redevelopment within a city need not be contiguous.

These facts allow a serious and, in our opinion, unlawful political abuse:

A development-oriented majority of a town council, sitting as a redevelopment agency, can include the home
of any "sane growth" minority member in a "proposed" "redevelopment" "study area", even though his or her
neighborhood is not "blighted" under the law, "study" the question of whether there is "blight" in his or her
neighborhood -- potentially resulting, under the Political Reform Act of 1974, in his or her total exclusion from
any discussion of the redevelopment and related growth issues, until such time -- much later in the process,
and after many crucial decisions have already been made -- as it is finally "determined" that his or her
neighborhood was not blighted after all.

This is the substance of what has happened to me in Half Moon Bay, and I am hereby appealing for both your
jurisdictional and political help in refranchising a great number of voters of Half Moon Bay who are
extremely worried about the future of their city.

We believe that in enacting by initiative the Political Reform Act of 1974, the California electorate never

FPPC                                    - 3 -                                    April 10, 1990

intended that legislation to apply to a situation such as mine, where the effect would be the complete eradication of potentially embarrassing questions put to development interests, and the complete political gutting of a "sane growth" constituency, amidst self-serving contentions by a development-oriented city council majority that city council members are elected by an entire city, and not, in any respect, by concerned citizen constituencies.

In the instant case, I live in a neighborhood called Arieta Park. It is important to note that Arieta Park and its surroundings are no more blighted than the respective neighborhoods of the other city council members of Half Moon Bay (-- and, again, without a legally sustainable finding of "blight", the use of the redevelopment agency device is illegal).

In fact, Arieta Park is a thriving residential neighborhood with new construction and high property values which have appreciated by an annual average in excess of 20% over the past three years.

In fact, home prices in Arieta Park presently range from approximately $250,000 to approximately $425,000.

California statutes and case law characterize "blighted" areas as those with a higher street crime rate, a higher arson rate, a higher percentage of unsafe structures, a higher percentage of dilapidated structures, etc..

Under California law, irregular parcelization cannot, in and of itself, support a finding of blight, nor can open spaces be considered blighted. Thus, the area near Arieta Park is not eligible for redevelopment either.

Nonetheless, other city officials recently (1) included my neighborhood, Arieta Park, in a proposed redevelopment "study" area, along with lands held by major development interests, (2) arranged for the FPPC to be contacted, and (3) angrily refused to hold the redevelopment process in abeyance until the FPPC formally opined on any eligibility to participate in redevelopment matters.

I ask the FPPC to carefully note that, at a recent redevelopment meeting, the city admitted that no written legal opinion of counsel exists which provides that my neighborhood, Arieta Park, is "blighted", under California law. Indeed, we believe there is no such written legal opinion of counsel, because describing Arieta Park as "blighted" would likely expose such counsel to charges of malpractice or worse at a later stage. We further believe that, in the manifest absence of "blight", allowing a redevelopment agency mechanism to proceed, consuming public resources, could also constitute malpractice.

In short, neither Arieta Park, nor any other area presently in the redevelopment "study" area is blighted, as blight is defined under California statute and case law, despite absurd arguments to the contrary, regarding irregular parcelization, etc., in the face of clear court rulings.

So far, regarding Arieta Park, the city council majority and certain advisers have hung their hats on an assertion that my neighborhood might benefit from improved sewers or sidewalks -- an assertion that applies equally well to most of Half Moon Bay (-- sewer improvement even being an announced city-wide goal). It has even been suggested that if Arieta Park residents do not go along with their inclusion in the redevelopment area, they could face assessments from a threatened assessment district for improvements to sewers, etc., even though Arieta Park citizens are hardly crying out for such "improvements".

Further, even if my neighborhood could benefit from fancier sewers, the economic life of its present sewers is not at an end, and, given the recent Arieta Park appreciation, the present sewers have obviously not ended my thriving neighborhood's overall economic use.

Please note that the city has not formally responded in detail to questions of the many citizens who have asked how Arieta Park could possibly be "blighted". Instead, the city's current strategy is to say that "engineers" will be sent in to "study" Arieta Park's sewers, etc., and then legal counsel will opine as to whether the "engineers" "findings" mean that Arieta Park is "blighted".

REQUEST

FPPC                                    - 4 -                                    April 10, 1990

It is time for such unfair political maneuverings to be seen for what they are.

Accordingly, I ask the Commission:

(1)     To declare that the Political Reform Act of 1974 was never intended to apply to facts such as these, especially where no formal written legal opinion of counsel exists claiming – and risking the malpractice, etc. of claiming – that my neighborhood is "blighted" – and that, therefore, I may participate in all redevelopment and related growth issues.

I respectfully submit that every interpreter of the literal wording of our statutes, and the rules promulgated thereunder, should continually ask the litmus test question of whether those statutes and rules were ever intended to apply to particular fact situations.

It seems to me that the absence of a formal written legal opinion of counsel that my neighborhood is "blighted", as defined by California law, is a crucial point upon which the FPPC can articulate a distinction in interpreting any existing law.

(2)     To recommend, if (1) is not seen presently possible, alternatives short of the utter and complete disfranchisement of my Half Moon Bay constituency, in line with existing FPPC pronouncements supporting alternatives short of the complete disfranchisement of voter constituencies.

(3)     For its jurisdictional and political support in ending all political maneuverings cloaked as conflict of interest issues, such as has occurred in my case.

(4)     To use any investigative powers it may have with respect to recent developments in Half Moon Bay.

On behalf of thousands of extremely concerned voters in Half Moon Bay, I especially ask for your jurisdictional and political help in ensuring that the pointed questions do get asked of development interests.

If they are not so asked, upon your next drive to the disappearing beauty of California's coast, you will be greeted by yet more abhorrent memorials to developer greed.

Many persons besides myself are seeking to preserve important coastal values, views, and life styles, while at the same time remaining sensitive to the concerns of others, including developers.

Development is not, per se, unacceptable -- but ugly, high-density, "hit and run" coastal development is.

I am among the many California citizens who applaud your efforts on behalf of fair practices in politics. Half Moon Bay and I now ask for your jurisdictional and political help.

Sincerely,

David Iverson

cc:     Senator Marian Bergeson
        Senator Quentin Kopp
        Assemblyman Ted Lempert
        Senator Rebecca Morgan
        Senate Committee On Local Government

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilmember who owns residential property within a proposed redevelopment project area may participate in decisions regarding adoption of the redevelopment plan, and whether the FPPC's preliminary guidance excluding him was legally and procedurally appropriate.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (January 23) and follow-up correspondence/appeal (April 10) challenging FPPC's preliminary guidance. Neither section constitutes an FPPC response or advice letter itself. The April 10 letter is partially truncated. Document ID 90067 matches the reference in the April 10 letter ('YOUR FILE NUMBER A-90-67'). High confidence on document type and structure; moderate confidence on synthesizing the councilmember's complex arguments in his April 10 letter.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether a city councilmember who owns residential property within a proposed redevelopment project area may participate in decisions regarding adoption of the redevelopment plan, and whether the FPPC's preliminary guidance excluding him was legally and procedurally appropriate.

The city has a total population of approximately 9,000 persons and is less than 10 square miles in size. The proposed project area represents about 10 or 15 percent of the existing residential area of the city. Unfortunately, not all facts were presented to Mr. Rothman. This letter places many omitted facts before you,
embedding.summary: This is a two-part document: an initial request letter (January 23, 1990) from attorney Iris P. Yang seeking FPPC advice on conflict of interest rules for a councilmember with property in a proposed redevelopment area, and a follow-up letter (April 10, 1990) from the councilmember himself challenging preliminary FPPC guidance and arguing his exclusion from redevelopment votes represents political disenfranchisement motivated by development interests seeking to silence a growth-skeptical council voice.
